title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered in a charity bookshop. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Frances, The Donations Sorter
Frances originally volunteered in a charity bookshop because she wanted somewhere quiet after leaving a noisy office where phones never stopped ringing. [47]She quickly learned that the first job was checking donated books for personal letters, pressed flowers and forgotten bank statements.[/47] It felt intrusive at first, but one returned photograph made the care worthwhile, because the owner recognised a childhood holiday. Frances had worked in records management, and [48]her old filing habits helped her create separate shelves for local history, exam guides and damaged stock awaiting decisions.[/48] The hardest part was refusing bags of books that smelled of damp or smoke, especially when donors had carried them across town. She began using gentler prepared explanations about protecting other stock. Frances says the shop changed her view of second-hand donated books: each copy arrived with evidence of another life, even before a customer opened it again on the bus home.
texts.B:
### Luke, The Window Designer
Luke carefully arranged the bookshop window every Monday morning before opening, often standing outside to judge the effect. [49]He stopped displaying only beautiful hardbacks after noticing that children's comics brought many more families through the door than prize-winning novels.[/49] His weekly displays became playful, with umbrellas for rainy-day holiday reading or maps for travel stories, depending on what donations had arrived. As a theatre lighting student, he understood how reflections on the glass could make a title invisible from the pavement at certain times of day. [50]His most successful window used cookbooks beside donated saucepans, which sold out faster than any literary theme.[/50] Luke enjoyed the freedom, but he found it difficult when committee members tried to add too many handwritten signs. He says a window has only three seconds to invite someone inside, so for him clutter is rarely kindness, however worthy the message or the campaign behind it.
texts.C:
### Amira, The Reading Group Host
Amira regularly hosted a monthly evening reading group in the shop's small upstairs back room after the shop closed. [51]She chose shorter books after one meeting where half the regular members admitted they had only reached only chapter three.[/51] Rather than criticising them, she began selecting new titles that could be discussed from a single powerful scene, character or moral choice. Amira worked as a community mediator, and [52]that experience helped her manage disagreements when readers strongly interpreted a character's decision very differently.[/52] Her favourite meeting was about a short crime novel, because a retired police officer and a teenager both changed their minds during the final conversation, surprising everyone. She served tea in mismatched donated mugs and insisted that nobody had to sound academic or quote reviews. Amira says the small group mattered because people who entered simply as customers gradually slowly learned one another's names and saved seats.
texts.D:
### Peter, The Online Seller
Peter photographed valuable donations for the shop's online account on quiet weekday afternoons. [53]He realised that placing a ruler beside rare books reduced complaints about size from distant buyers.[/53] The task required patience because every description had to include condition, edition and postage weight before anything could be advertised. Peter had been a postal worker, so he was unusually good at predicting which parcels needed extra protection in the mail. [54]His worst error was listing a signed book too cheaply because he failed to recognise the author's married name.[/54] After that, he checked unusual inscriptions against a reference database. He rarely met customers, which sometimes made the work feel detached from the shop's friendly atmosphere downstairs. [55]He now leaves a weekly note explaining which online sales funded specific local projects.[/55] [56]He says the best reward is knowing a forgotten book can travel to the one reader actively searching for it.[/56]

questions.question: had to check donations for private items left inside them?
questions.explanation: Frances checked donated books for letters, pressed flowers and bank statements.

questions.question: used previous records experience to organise categories of stock?
questions.explanation: Frances's filing habits helped her create separate shelves for different types of books.

questions.question: changed displays after noticing what attracted a particular type of visitor?
questions.explanation: Luke displayed comics after seeing they brought more families into the shop.

questions.question: created a display combining books with related household objects?
questions.explanation: Luke's successful window used cookbooks beside donated saucepans.

questions.question: adapted choices after discovering people had not finished the reading?
questions.explanation: Amira chose shorter books after members admitted reaching only chapter three.

questions.question: used conflict-management experience when people disagreed about a story?
questions.explanation: Amira's mediation background helped her manage differing interpretations.

questions.question: included a measuring object in photographs to prevent buyer complaints?
questions.explanation: Peter placed a ruler beside rare books to reduce complaints about size.

questions.question: made a pricing mistake because an author's identity was not obvious?
questions.explanation: Peter listed a signed book too cheaply because he missed the author's married name.

questions.question: connects remote sales with the local benefits they support?
questions.explanation: Peter leaves notes explaining which online sales funded local projects.

questions.question: values matching an overlooked item with someone specifically seeking it?
questions.explanation: Peter likes that a forgotten book can reach the reader searching for it.
